flashcards.front: What is the primary purpose of cache memory in a computer system?
flashcards.back: The primary purpose of cache memory is to act as a high-speed buffer between the CPU and the slower main memory (RAM). It stores frequently accessed data and instructions to reduce the average time (latency) to access data from the main memory.

The Principle of Locality is the observation that programs tend to access data and instructions in a non-uniform way. It has two types: Temporal Locality (recently accessed items are likely to be accessed again soon) and Spatial Locality (items near recently accessed items are likely to be accessed soon). This principle is the fundamental reason why cache memory is effective.

flashcards.front: What are the three common cache mapping techniques?
flashcards.back: The three common cache mapping techniques are: 1) Direct Mapped (each main memory block maps to exactly one cache line), 2) Fully Associative (a memory block can be placed in any cache line), and 3) Set Associative (a compromise where the cache is divided into sets, and a memory block maps to any line within a specific set).

flashcards.front: What is a cache hit vs. a cache miss?
flashcards.back: A Cache Hit occurs when the data requested by the CPU is found in the cache. A Cache Miss occurs when the requested data is not in the cache and must be fetched from the main memory. The performance goal is to maximize hit rate and minimize miss rate.

flashcards.front: What is the typical memory hierarchy in terms of speed, size, and cost?
flashcards.back: The memory hierarchy, from fastest/smallest/most expensive to slowest/largest/cheapest, is: CPU Registers → Level 1 (L1) Cache → Level 2 (L2) Cache → Level 3 (L3) Cache → Main Memory (RAM) → Secondary Storage (e.g., SSD/HDD). Cache memory bridges the speed gap between CPU and main memory.
